whatItIs: Apple's background-activity scheduler: decides when deferrable system work (App Store updates, backups, analytics uploads) should run based on power, thermal, and usage predictions.
whenHotImplies: Almost always a scheduling loop that has got stuck, often on one task it keeps trying to line up (check appstoreagent too). Steady high CPU, or memory in the multi-GB range, is never normal for something whose only job is scheduling. On macOS 27 betas specifically, that multi-GB memory growth is a known BiomeAgent glitch: BiomeAgent keeps re-registering an Apple Intelligence task over and over, and dasd piles up memory keeping track of it — climbing into the tens of GB.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up fresh within seconds, which clears the built-up memory right away.
worstCase: A background task like a backup or update check runs a little later than it otherwise would.

whatItIs: Part of Biome, Apple's on-device stream store for behavioral and contextual signals that feed Siri Suggestions and Apple Intelligence. BiomeAgent registers and coordinates background tasks that process these signals.
whenHotImplies: On macOS 27 betas, a known glitch makes BiomeAgent keep re-registering the same Apple Intelligence task over and over; what you usually see is dasd's memory ballooning into the tens of GB rather than BiomeAgent itself running hot. It's been reported to Apple as a beta bug.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up on its own; quitting dasd is what actually clears the built-up memory. If it keeps coming back on a beta, the deeper fix (which does affect Siri and Apple Intelligence) is to turn BiomeAgent off — that's reversible, and worth turning back on once Apple fixes it.
worstCase: Siri Suggestions and some Apple Intelligence context may briefly stop updating until it starts back up.

whatItIs: The MySQL (or Percona) database server. Hosts databases for local development and services; commonly installed via Homebrew, Docker, or a native package.
ownedBy: The MySQL/Percona database server. Ships via Homebrew, a native installer package, or Docker. Its runtime owner and install source vary by install — Anomalous reads those live, per process.
whenHotImplies: High CPU is almost always a CLIENT hammering the database, not mysqld itself: a busy-polling job queue (Laravel/Sidekiq workers with a near-zero sleep), an N+1 query storm, a missing index, or a runaway migration. A telling signature is thousands of individually sub-millisecond queries per second — each looks idle in SHOW PROCESSLIST, but the aggregate rate pins a core (death by a million paper cuts). Find the client; check SHOW FULL PROCESSLIST and your app's queue workers.
worstCase: Please don't quit it just to quiet the CPU — that would drop every app's database connections and can lose writes that are still in progress, and the load comes right back the moment the client reconnects. Fix the client causing the load instead (for example, a queue worker started with --sleep=0).

whatItIs: The MariaDB database server (a MySQL fork). Same role and behavior as mysqld for local development and services.
ownedBy: The MariaDB database server. Ships via Homebrew, a native package, or Docker; the runtime owner varies by install — Anomalous reads it live, per process.
whenHotImplies: As with mysqld: high CPU is almost always a client hammering it — busy-polling queue workers, N+1 queries, missing indexes, or a runaway query. The fix is on the client side.
worstCase: Quitting it drops all database connections and risks writes that are still in progress; the load returns when the client reconnects. Fix the client, not the server.

whatItIs: A PostgreSQL backend process. Postgres runs one process per connection plus background workers (autovacuum, checkpointer, WAL writer), so several 'postgres' processes are normal.
ownedBy: The PostgreSQL database server. Ships via Homebrew, Postgres.app, a native package, or Docker; the runtime owner varies by install — Anomalous reads it live, per process.
whenHotImplies: A hot backend usually means one expensive query or a client connection doing heavy work; a hot autovacuum worker means a large table is being vacuumed. Identify the query via pg_stat_activity rather than quitting the process.
worstCase: Quitting a backend drops that client's transaction; quitting the main postgres process takes the whole database down. Diagnose the query instead.

whatItIs: An in-memory key-value store used for caching, queues, and sessions. Single-threaded for command execution.
ownedBy: The Redis in-memory data store. Ships via Homebrew, a native package, or Docker; the runtime owner varies by install — Anomalous reads it live, per process.
whenHotImplies: Sustained CPU means a client is issuing expensive commands (KEYS on a large keyspace, big sorts, hot Lua scripts) or an extremely high command rate. Growing memory can mean an unbounded key set with no eviction policy. Check the slow log and MONITOR briefly.
worstCase: Quitting it drops all cached data and any queue that isn't saved to disk; apps relying on it will error until it restarts. Fix the client's command pattern.

whatItIs: The MongoDB database server.
ownedBy: The MongoDB database server. Ships via Homebrew, a native package, or Docker; the runtime owner varies by install — Anomalous reads it live, per process.
whenHotImplies: High CPU usually means an unindexed query doing a collection scan, or a heavy aggregation pipeline. Check db.currentOp() and slow-query logs for the offending operation.
worstCase: Quitting it drops connections and risks writes that are still in progress. Diagnose the query, don't stop the server.

whatItIs: A local large-language-model server that runs open models on your machine.
whenHotImplies: High CPU/GPU and large memory are EXPECTED while a model is loaded and generating — that's the actual work, not a runaway. It's only a concern if it stays maxed out long after you've stopped using it (a stuck request), or memory never frees up between runs.
safeAction: If it's sitting idle but still running hot, quitting Ollama is safe — it reloads the model on the next request.
worstCase: You interrupt an in-progress generation; nothing is lost beyond that request.

whatItIs: The Docker Desktop virtual-machine backend that runs your containers on macOS. Container CPU and memory show up here because they run inside its VM.
whenHotImplies: Sustained CPU is usually a container inside it working hard (a build, a busy service, a filesystem-watch loop over a bind mount). Inspect with `docker stats` to find the container; the backend itself is rarely the culprit.
safeAction: prefer stopping the specific hot container (docker stop) over quitting Docker Desktop
worstCase: Quitting Docker stops every running container at once.

whatItIs: Handles App Store background operations: update checks, downloads, receipt validation.
whenHotImplies: A stuck background update, or a download that keeps retrying. Often it's the very task dasd is stuck repeating when both are running hot.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up automatically, and any interrupted download picks back up.
worstCase: An app update arrives on the next check instead of now.

whatItIs: Spotlight's index-writing worker. Reads changed files and updates the search index.
whenHotImplies: Heavy indexing after lots of files changed at once (big code checkouts, mail imports, migrations) — often perfectly normal, just long-running. Ongoing heat when nothing's changing on disk suggests the index is corrupt.
safeAction: usually wait; if persistent, rebuild the index for the hot volume (sudo mdutil -E)
worstCase: Interrupting mid-index restarts that volume's indexing from scratch.

whatItIs: The kernel itself — the core of macOS. Important: high kernel_task CPU is macOS deliberately keeping the processor busy on purpose to cool the Mac down — it's the SYMPTOM of heat, not the cause.
ownedBy: Apple (the kernel — cannot and must not be killed)
whenHotImplies: Your Mac is hot. The correct diagnosis names WHO is heating it (look at the other anomalies in this window), plus airflow/charger-side factors.
worstCase: Not applicable — this one can't be quit, by design.

whatItIs: Analyzes photos and videos on-device: faces, objects, scenes, OCR for Photos search and Visual Look Up.
whenHotImplies: Working through a large or newly imported photo library, usually on AC power overnight. Legitimate but can run hot for hours; runaway if it persists for days on an unchanged library.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up on its own and it picks the analysis up again later.
worstCase: Photos search/People albums update later.

whatItIs: Performs Time Machine backups.
whenHotImplies: A large backup in progress (first backup, big churn) — usually legitimate. Sustained heat with tiny backups suggests a snapshot or destination problem.
safeAction: Better to stop the backup from Time Machine settings rather than quitting it.
worstCase: Quitting it mid-backup can leave an incomplete snapshot to clean up (macOS handles that automatically, but the backup starts over).

whatItIs: Syncs iCloud Drive and Desktop & Documents folders.
whenHotImplies: Large sync in progress, or a sync conflict loop (same file re-uploading endlessly — check console for repeated items).
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up on its own and it picks the sync back up.
worstCase: Sync pauses for a moment; nothing is lost — the copy on the server is the master.

whatItIs: Composites every window on screen. All drawing flows through it.
whenHotImplies: Some app is forcing constant redraws (animating dashboards, broken transparency), or too many high-refresh displays. WindowServer heat is almost always ANOTHER app's fault.
worstCase: Quitting it logs you out of the entire session instantly, losing unsaved work everywhere.

whatItIs: Enforces Gatekeeper: notarization checks, first-launch app assessment, executable policy.
whenHotImplies: Assessing a large newly-installed app bundle, or repeatedly re-assessing unsigned binaries (common with large dev trees).
worstCase: Security assessment isn't something to quit — best to wait it out.

whatItIs: Certificate trust evaluation for TLS and code signing across the system.
whenHotImplies: A burst of trust evaluations — often an app opening many TLS connections, or revocation-check storms on a bad network.
worstCase: System-critical; heat is a symptom of a chatty client, not trustd.

whatItIs: The core of Spotlight. Coordinates indexing, spawns the mdworker processes, and answers search queries against the metadata index.
whenHotImplies: Managing a big indexing job, or working in circles because a volume's index is corrupt and it keeps handing out the same work. Its helper processes (mdworker/mdworker_shared) usually show the actual CPU.
safeAction: Better to use mdutil rather than quitting it; if the index is corrupt, rebuild it with sudo mdutil -E on the affected volume.
worstCase: Quitting it interrupts indexing and search for a moment; macOS starts it back up on its own.

whatItIs: A short-lived Spotlight worker that opens changed files and runs the matching importer to pull out searchable text and details. Shared, sandboxed copies handle many file types.
whenHotImplies: Actively indexing new or changed files — usually a big import, download, or code checkout. A single mdworker_shared stuck running for a long time often means one problem file its importer can't handle (a huge or malformed document).
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — Spotlight starts its helpers back up as needed, and that file gets re-indexed later.
worstCase: One file's metadata is extracted a bit later; search stays otherwise intact.

whatItIs: The Spotlight worker process that extracts metadata and text from files for the search index. The non-shared form runs importers that need their own process.
whenHotImplies: Same as its shared sibling: lots of changed files being indexed, or a single file whose importer is stuck. Many mdworkers at once is normal during a big import.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — Spotlight starts it back up, and the file gets re-indexed later.
worstCase: One file's metadata lands in the index slightly later.

whatItIs: Indexes content that apps donate to Spotlight through the Core Spotlight API — Mail messages, Messages, Notes, and third-party app items — as opposed to files on disk.
whenHotImplies: An app is handing over or re-indexing a big batch of items (a large Mail account resync, a Notes migration). Ongoing heat can mean it's stuck re-indexing the same app's data over and over.
safeAction: Usually best to wait; it settles once the app finishes, or after an index rebuild.
worstCase: In-app Spotlight results (Mail, Notes) update later; on-disk file search is unaffected.

whatItIs: Records a log of directory changes so services like Spotlight and Time Machine can learn what changed without rescanning the whole disk.
whenHotImplies: A flood of filesystem changes — a giant build, a bulk copy, or a runaway process writing endlessly. fseventsd is rarely the culprit; it's just reflecting whatever is hammering the disk.
worstCase: System-critical for change tracking; quitting it can make Spotlight and Time Machine miss changes and force full rescans.

whatItIs: Runs the Photos library's background analysis — face recognition, scene and object detection, and building the People and Memories features — while Photos is not in the foreground.
whenHotImplies: Working through a large or freshly imported library, typically when idle and on power. Normal for hours after a big import; suspicious if it runs for days on an unchanged library.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up on its own and picks the analysis up again later, usually overnight while plugged in.
worstCase: People albums, Memories, and photo search update later.

whatItIs: Manages the Photos library database — imports, edits, album membership, and iCloud Photos sync bookkeeping.
whenHotImplies: A large import, edit, or iCloud Photos sync in progress. Sustained heat with no library activity can indicate a database that needs repair.
safeAction: Quit Photos and let it settle rather than force-quitting this — the library is best not interrupted while it's writing.
worstCase: Interrupting a library write can require Photos to repair the library on next launch.

whatItIs: The system audio server. Every sound in and out of the Mac — playback, recording, device switching — flows through it.
whenHotImplies: Almost always an app hammering the audio stack (a stuck stream, a plug-in in a tight loop, constant sample-rate switching) rather than coreaudiod itself. Look for the audio app that is actually busy.
worstCase: System audio infrastructure; quitting it cuts all audio and can jam up apps that are holding an audio session.

whatItIs: Syncs CloudKit data for iCloud and for apps that store data in iCloud — the transport behind iCloud-backed app databases.
whenHotImplies: A large iCloud sync, or a burst of retries when an app's iCloud data keeps failing to line up. Often runs hot alongside the app whose data it's syncing.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up on its own and it picks the sync back up; your data lives on the server, so nothing is lost.
worstCase: iCloud-backed app data syncs a little later; no local data loss.

whatItIs: Coordinates File Provider extensions — the on-demand file system behind iCloud Drive and third-party clients like Dropbox, OneDrive, and Google Drive.
whenHotImplies: Downloading or clearing out many files, or a third-party cloud extension misbehaving in a sync loop. Check which cloud app is active.
safeAction: Better to pause the cloud app that's acting up — quitting this can interrupt a file that's mid-download.
worstCase: A file that was downloading on demand has to start over; the cloud apps generally recover.

whatItIs: Performs background downloads and uploads on behalf of the system and apps — the transfer engine many services hand their traffic to so it continues when the app isn't running.
whenHotImplies: A large background transfer, or a client that keeps re-queuing a failing download. The heat is bandwidth and retry work for whichever service owns the transfer.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up, and background transfers pick up from where they left off.
worstCase: An in-progress download or upload pauses briefly, then resumes.

whatItIs: Handles account sync for Exchange and other server accounts — Mail, Contacts, and Calendar over ActiveSync/CardDAV/CalDAV.
whenHotImplies: A big mailbox resync, or a sync loop against a server that keeps returning errors (a classic: one item the server and Mac disagree on). Persistent heat usually points at one bad account.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up and it picks up where it left off; your mail lives on the server, so nothing is lost.
worstCase: Mail/Contacts/Calendar sync pauses briefly for that account.

whatItIs: Syncs calendars and manages calendar accounts (iCloud and CalDAV), fetching and pushing events in the background.
whenHotImplies: A large calendar resync, or a loop over a repeating event or invitation where one broken event keeps failing to sync with the server.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — it restarts and picks up where it left off; your calendar lives on the server, so nothing is lost.
worstCase: Calendar changes sync a little later.

whatItIs: Backs Apple Maps: fetches map tiles, geocoding, and routing data, and caches them for the Maps app and location-aware features.
whenHotImplies: Active Maps use downloading tiles and routes. Idle heat is unusual and can mean a cache or download it keeps retrying.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up, and Maps reloads whatever it needs.
worstCase: Maps briefly re-fetches tiles or route data.

whatItIs: Downloads and manages on-demand Apple assets — Siri voices, dictionaries, fonts, keyboard and ML models, and other 'MobileAsset' bundles delivered outside the main OS update.
whenHotImplies: Fetching a batch of assets after a login or OS update, or retrying a download that keeps failing. Often paired with a background download service.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up and it picks up where it left off; the downloads retry later.
worstCase: An optional asset (a voice, a dictionary) arrives later.

whatItIs: Powers proactive Siri Suggestions — learning from your Mail, Contacts, and app usage to surface names, events, and links, and to detect data like addresses in text.
whenHotImplies: Processing a burst of new content (a big Mail import, lots of new messages) to update suggestions. Sustained heat can mean it is chewing on a large or problematic data set.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up and it picks the learning up again later.
worstCase: Siri Suggestions refresh a bit later.

whatItIs: Builds the on-device knowledge graph from your data — linking people, places, and events mined from Mail, Messages, and Contacts to feed Siri and Spotlight suggestions.
whenHotImplies: Taking in a big batch of new personal data (a fresh Mail or Messages sync) to rebuild the graph. Usually settles once it's caught up; long-lasting heat can mean it keeps reprocessing the same data.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up and it picks up where it left off; the graph rebuilds a bit at a time.
worstCase: Suggestions and Spotlight relevance update later.

whatItIs: Collects and models usage patterns — app launches, activity, and context — to drive app predictions, Handoff, and proactive suggestions, and to inform the activity scheduler.
whenHotImplies: Working through a backlog of activity data. Ongoing heat can mean its data store has grown bloated or got stuck, and it keeps re-scanning it.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up and it picks up where it left off; predictions rebuild over time.
worstCase: App predictions and proactive suggestions are briefly less accurate.

whatItIs: Part of Biome, Apple's on-device stream of user-activity events used by Siri and proactive features. biomesyncd syncs that data across your signed-in devices.
whenHotImplies: Catching up a backlog of activity events across your devices, or a sync loop when one device's Biome data is out of step. A known occasional CPU hog after setup or a restore.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up and it picks the sync back up.
worstCase: Cross-device activity data syncs later; no user-visible data loss.

whatItIs: Runs Continuity sharing features — AirDrop, Handoff, Universal Clipboard, Instant Hotspot, and shared-media discovery.
whenHotImplies: Actively looking for nearby devices over Wi-Fi and Bluetooth (an AirDrop window open, nearby devices announcing themselves), or a loop when Bluetooth or Wi-Fi keeps flipping on and off.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up, and the sharing features come right back.
worstCase: AirDrop/Handoff drop for a moment, then recover.

whatItIs: Enables Continuity connectivity between your Apple devices — the BLE/Wi-Fi link behind Handoff, iPhone Cellular Calls, Instant Hotspot, and Sidecar.
whenHotImplies: Constantly looking for and announcing itself to nearby devices. Steady heat often tracks Bluetooth or Wi-Fi acting up, or a nearby device that keeps connecting and dropping.
safeAction: Quitting it is safe — macOS starts it back up and reconnects the Continuity links.
worstCase: Handoff and cross-device calling blink out briefly.

whatItIs: Manages Apple's identity layer for iMessage, FaceTime, and Continuity — device registration and the encryption keys those services use.
whenHotImplies: Re-registering identities or sorting out accounts, often after a sign-in change or a network hiccup. Long-lasting heat can mean an account is stuck retrying its registration.
safeAction: Better to sign the affected account out and back in — quitting this disrupts Messages and FaceTime while they're mid-session.
worstCase: iMessage and FaceTime briefly lose registration and re-establish it.

whatItIs: The iMessage engine — sends and receives Messages and maintains their connection state.
whenHotImplies: Processing a large Messages backlog or attachment sync, or a send/receive retry loop against a struggling account.
safeAction: Better to quit Messages itself — quitting imagent drops any message that's mid-send along with the connection.
worstCase: A message in the middle of sending may need resending; history is safe.

whatItIs: Tracks call state for FaceTime and Continuity phone calls — routing, and relaying iPhone calls to the Mac.
whenHotImplies: Managing an active or ringing call, or a relay loop when the paired iPhone connection is unstable. Idle heat is unusual.
safeAction: Better to end the call normally — quitting this can drop a call that's in progress.
worstCase: An active FaceTime or relayed phone call is dropped.

whatItIs: The FaceTime audio/video engine — captures, encodes, and streams the media for FaceTime calls.
whenHotImplies: An active FaceTime call — high CPU here is expected during a call. Heat with no call running is the odd part, and suggests a call that didn't fully end.
safeAction: Better to end the FaceTime call itself — quitting this drops the call right away.
worstCase: The active FaceTime call ends abruptly.

whatItIs: Apple's built-in malware scanner. Runs periodic background scans (each scan module has its own XProtectRemediator process) to detect and remove known malware families.
whenHotImplies: A scheduled malware scan in progress — legitimate and periodic, and can briefly use real CPU and disk. Suspicious only if it runs continuously rather than in short bursts.
safeAction: Better to let the scan finish — it's harmless and will simply run again if you quit it, but it's doing real security work.
worstCase: A malware scan is skipped and simply runs again on the next schedule.

whatItIs: Manages macOS software updates — checking for, downloading, and preparing system updates.
whenHotImplies: An update check, download, or 'preparing' phase underway. Sustained heat can mean a download that keeps restarting or an update stuck preparing.
safeAction: Better to manage it from System Settings > General > Software Update — quitting this interrupts the download or prep, though it does resume.
worstCase: An in-progress update download or preparation restarts.

whatItIs: Enforces Transparency, Consent, and Control — the privacy permissions for Camera, Microphone, Screen Recording, Files, and Accessibility, including the consent prompts.
whenHotImplies: A burst of permission checks, often an app repeatedly probing an access it lacks. tccd is enforcing, not the runaway — find the app triggering the checks.
worstCase: Privacy-critical; quitting it can break permission prompts and leave apps unable to get or check access.

whatItIs: Validates code signatures when executables and libraries load — a core part of system integrity and Gatekeeper enforcement.
whenHotImplies: A wave of first-time code-signature validations — launching many new binaries at once, or a large unsigned/ad-hoc-signed dev tree. It is verifying whoever is launching, not looping on its own.
worstCase: Security-critical; quitting it can block apps from launching and destabilize the system.

whatItIs: Performs app and package installation work in the background.
whenHotImplies: An install or update actively unpacking and writing files. Sustained heat can mean an install stuck retrying or working through a very large package.
worstCase: Quitting it mid-install can leave a half-installed app that has to be reinstalled — best not to interrupt it.

whatItIs: Manages sleep, wake, display dimming, and power assertions — the arbiter of when the Mac may idle-sleep.
whenHotImplies: Rarely hot itself; when it is, some component is rapidly toggling power requests (over and over preventing and then allowing sleep). The fix is whatever is holding those requests, not powerd.
worstCase: System-critical; quitting it disrupts sleep, wake, and power management.

whatItIs: The system watchdog — monitors for hung critical processes and can force a restart if the system stops responding.
whenHotImplies: Elevated activity can mean it is tracking a process that is hanging or missing its checkpoints. It is the watcher, not the runaway.
worstCase: System-critical; never quit it — it's what keeps the system responsive.

whatItIs: Coordinates mounting and unmounting of disks and volumes, and notifies apps when storage appears or disappears.
whenHotImplies: A rapid series of mount/unmount events — a flaky external drive reconnecting, or disk images attaching over and over. It's reacting to all that activity, not causing it.
worstCase: System-critical for volume management; quitting it can jam up mounting and ejecting drives.

whatItIs: The lightweight system notification hub — passes small 'something changed' signals between processes. Used constantly by the whole system.
whenHotImplies: A process posting or observing notifications in a tight loop, flooding the hub. The heat is a chatty client; notifyd is just the switchboard.
worstCase: Extremely central; quitting it destabilizes the many processes that rely on these notifications.

whatItIs: Reads and writes app and system preferences (settings files), handling one request at a time for each app's settings.
whenHotImplies: An app hammering its preferences — saving settings over and over in a loop. cfprefsd is just doing the reading and writing for a misbehaving app; find the app that keeps changing its settings.
worstCase: Quitting it during a write risks losing or corrupting the settings for any app saving at that moment.

whatItIs: Resolves users, groups, and authentication — local accounts and any network directory (LDAP/Active Directory) the Mac is bound to.
whenHotImplies: A flood of identity lookups or auth attempts, or slow/failing queries against a bound network directory. On networked Macs, a slow directory server is a common cause.
worstCase: Security- and login-critical; quitting it can break sign-in and account lookups.

whatItIs: The input handler — processes what comes from keyboards, mice, trackpads, and other input devices.
whenHotImplies: A device sending a flood of input — a stuck key, a jittery mouse or trackpad, or a misbehaving accessory. The device is the cause, not hidd.
worstCase: Input-critical; quitting it can leave the Mac unresponsive to the keyboard and pointer.

whatItIs: Collects and stores the unified system log that every process writes to, and serves it to tools like Console and the log command.
whenHotImplies: Some process is logging at a furious rate, flooding the log stream. logd's heat is the volume of a chatty component — hunt the process spamming the log.
worstCase: Quitting it disrupts logging for the whole system; it comes right back, but the log history around the gap is lost.

whatItIs: Backs Launch Services — the registry of installed apps, their document and URL type handlers, and the 'Open With' associations.
whenHotImplies: Rebuilding app registrations after installs or an OS update, or a loop re-registering a malformed app bundle. Often hot right after large app changes.
safeAction: Usually best to wait; quitting it does bring it right back, but app launching and icon lookups hiccup for a moment.
worstCase: File/URL associations and app launching hiccup while it rebuilds.

whatItIs: Manages Apple ID authentication and account state — the tokens and sign-in status that iCloud and Apple services depend on.
whenHotImplies: Re-signing in or sorting out your Apple ID, often after a password change or a network hiccup. Long-lasting heat can mean it's stuck retrying the sign-in.
safeAction: Better to sort the Apple ID sign-in in System Settings — stopping it now can interrupt a sign-in already in progress.
worstCase: Apple ID-backed services briefly lose their sign-in, then re-establish it.

whatItIs: Maintains the persistent connection to Apple Push Notification service and fans incoming pushes out to apps and system services (Mail, Messages, iCloud).
whenHotImplies: A reconnect loop on a flaky network, or a flood of incoming pushes. Sustained heat often tracks network instability rather than apsd itself.
safeAction: Better to wait, or toggle your network — stopping it drops the notifications connection until it reconnects.
worstCase: Push notifications (Messages, Mail, iCloud) are delayed briefly while the connection re-establishes.

whatItIs: Generates a crash report when an app or process crashes, capturing the stack for diagnostics.
whenHotImplies: Something is crashing repeatedly — ReportCrash spikes each time it writes a report. Constant activity means an app is crash-looping; the fix is that app, not ReportCrash.
safeAction: Quitting it is harmless — macOS runs it again the next time it's needed.
worstCase: One crash report is not written; the crashing app is the real issue to address.

whatItIs: Samples unresponsive processes to produce a 'spindump' report the system uses to diagnose hangs and beachballs.
whenHotImplies: The system is sampling a process that is hanging — spindump running means something else is stuck (a beachballing app). It is the diagnostician, not the culprit.
safeAction: Quitting it is harmless — it runs again the next time something hangs.
worstCase: One hang report is skipped; the hanging app is the real target.

whatItIs: Apple's Messages app — iMessage and SMS.
whenHotImplies: Active syncing, a busy conversation, or a large attachment loading. Sustained high CPU or memory is usually a stuck send or a huge thread; quitting and reopening clears it.
safeAction: Quit and reopen Messages.
worstCase: You'll close your open conversations; nothing is lost — messages are synced.

whatItIs: Apple Mail — the built-in email client.
whenHotImplies: Indexing a large mailbox, downloading attachments, or a rebuild after an account change. Prolonged high CPU can mean a corrupt mailbox re-indexing.
safeAction: Quit and reopen Mail; if it persists, rebuild the mailbox (Mailbox ▸ Rebuild).
worstCase: You'll close Mail; drafts in progress are saved automatically.

whatItIs: Apple's Safari web browser.
whenHotImplies: A heavy page, a runaway script, or many open tabs. High CPU usually traces to one tab (a video, an ad, or a stuck web app).
safeAction: Close the heavy tab, or quit and reopen Safari.
worstCase: You'll lose your open tabs unless Safari reopens them; pinned tabs return.

whatItIs: Google's Chrome web browser.
ownedBy: Ships from Google (not part of macOS)
whenHotImplies: A heavy tab, an extension, or many tabs. Chrome spreads work across helper processes — the culprit is usually one tab or extension.
safeAction: Use Chrome's own Task Manager (Window ▸ Task Manager) to find and close the heavy tab.
worstCase: Closing a tab loses its unsaved page state; the browser stays open.

whatItIs: A Chrome renderer — one tab, frame, or extension running in its own process.
ownedBy: Ships from Google (not part of macOS)
whenHotImplies: A busy tab: video, animation, a heavy web app, or an ad. Elevated wake-ups and CPU here are normal for an active tab.
safeAction: Find the matching tab in Chrome's Task Manager and close it.
worstCase: You lose that tab's unsaved state; the rest of Chrome is unaffected.

whatItIs: The Slack messaging client (an Electron app).
ownedBy: Ships from Slack (not part of macOS)
whenHotImplies: Loading a busy workspace, a call, or many unread channels. Electron apps idle heavier than native ones; sustained high CPU can mean a stuck call or a huge channel.
safeAction: Quit and reopen Slack, or reload the workspace (Cmd-R).
worstCase: You'll close Slack; unsent drafts are kept.

whatItIs: The Zoom video-conferencing app.
ownedBy: Ships from Zoom (not part of macOS)
whenHotImplies: An active call — video encoding is legitimately CPU-heavy. Outside a call, sustained high CPU is unusual and worth a restart.
safeAction: If you're not in a call, quit and reopen Zoom.
worstCase: Quitting mid-call ends the call for you.

whatItIs: Visual Studio Code — Microsoft's code editor (an Electron app).
ownedBy: Ships from Microsoft (not part of macOS)
whenHotImplies: Indexing a large project, a busy extension, or a language server. High CPU often traces to one extension or a big file.
safeAction: Reload the window (Cmd-Shift-P ▸ Reload Window); disable a suspect extension if it recurs.
worstCase: Reloading closes unsaved editors' undo history; files on disk are safe.

whatItIs: The Spotify music client (an Electron app).
ownedBy: Ships from Spotify (not part of macOS)
whenHotImplies: Playback, downloading, or scanning a large local library. Sustained high CPU while idle is unusual.
safeAction: Quit and reopen Spotify.
worstCase: Playback stops; nothing is lost.

whatItIs: Apple Notes — the built-in note-taking app.
whenHotImplies: Syncing many notes or loading large attachments. Prolonged high CPU can mean a sync conflict.
safeAction: Quit and reopen Notes.
worstCase: You'll close Notes; edits are saved and synced.

whatItIs: Apple Photos — the built-in photo library.
whenHotImplies: Analyzing the library (faces, scenes), syncing iCloud, or generating thumbnails after an import. This is normal after adding photos and settles on its own.
safeAction: Let it finish; it's usually one-time work. If stuck for hours, quit and reopen Photos.
worstCase: You'll close Photos; the analysis resumes later.

whatItIs: Apple Music — the built-in music app.
whenHotImplies: Syncing the library, downloading, or updating cloud match. Sustained high CPU while idle is unusual.
safeAction: Quit and reopen Music.
worstCase: Playback stops; nothing is lost.

whatItIs: The macOS Finder — the desktop, windows, and file management.
whenHotImplies: Copying many files, a slow network volume, or a stuck window. Finder relaunches cleanly, so it's safe to restart.
safeAction: Relaunch Finder (hold Option, right-click the Dock icon ▸ Relaunch).
worstCase: Open Finder windows close and reopen; nothing is lost.

whatItIs: Docker Desktop — the container runtime UI and its Linux VM.
ownedBy: Ships from Docker (not part of macOS)
whenHotImplies: A busy container, a build, or the background VM. Docker's VM can hold significant CPU/memory; a runaway container is the usual cause.
safeAction: Check running containers (docker stats); stop the runaway one, or quit Docker Desktop.
worstCase: Quitting stops all your containers.

whatItIs: Apple's FaceTime video-calling app.
whenHotImplies: An active call — video is CPU-heavy. Outside a call, high CPU is unusual.
safeAction: If you're not in a call, quit and reopen FaceTime.
worstCase: Quitting mid-call ends the call.
